content.problemStatement:
Chef is organizing an online card game tournament and for this purpose he has to provide a card shuffling software. This software has to simulate the following shuffling process. A stack of N cards is placed face down on the table. Cards in the stack are ordered by value. Topmost card has value 1 and the one on the bottom has value N. To shuffle the cards we repeat the following steps M times:


take A cards from the top of the deck.
take another B cards from the top of the deck.
put the A cards, which you removed in the first step, back on top of the remaining deck.
take C cards from the deck
put the B cards, which you're still holding from the second move, **card by card** on top of the deck.
finally, return the block of C cards on top
constraints: Ai + Bi + Ci ≤ N for every move (the described cuts are always possible)
Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 100000
Sum of M over all test cases ≤ 100000
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T – the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and M – the number of cards and the number of shuffling moves. The next M lines each contain three integers Ai, Bi, Ci describing one move.
All cards are initially ordered from top to bottom as 1,2,…,N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the values of the cards from top to bottom after performing all M moves. Separate the numbers by a single space.

solution.approach:
The deck is a mutable sequence. Each move consists of cutting three consecutive blocks (A, B, the rest), putting A back, cutting a prefix C, reversing block B and finally concatenating the pieces as X + reverse(B) + Y where X is the first C cards after A has been restored. All operations are *cut‑and‑paste* on contiguous intervals, which can be performed in O(log N) using an implicit treap (a randomized binary search tree keyed by position). The treap stores the card value, subtree size, and a lazy flag for reversal. For a move we:
1. split(root, A) → (tA, rest1)
2. split(rest1, B) → (tB, tRest)
3. root = merge(tA, tRest)               // A is placed back on top
4. split(root, C) → (tX, tY)            // first C cards after step 3
5. pushReverse(tB)                       // lazy reversal of B
6. root = merge( merge(tX, tB), tY )    // final order for this move
After processing all moves we perform an inorder traversal of the treap to obtain the final deck.
The treap guarantees O((N+M)·log N) time and O(N) memory, which satisfies the limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + M) · log N) per test case – each split/merge is logarithmic.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – one node per card plus recursion stack.
